situation: A prime broker notices a family-office client has built huge single-stock exposure through swaps across several banks and keeps breaching its margin limits.
A tech-focused bank parked deposits in long-dated bonds that are now underwater, and its concentrated startup depositors are pulling funds fast as rates rise.
A jeweller obtained guarantees from a bank branch that were issued off-system without collateral and rolled them over for years undetected.
A private bank aggressively grew loans to stressed corporate groups, under-reported bad assets, and now faces a depositor run and a regulator-led rescue.
An infrastructure lender financed long-gestation projects with short-term borrowings, defaulted on its debt, and triggered contagion fears across shadow banks.
A housing-finance company is accused of diverting loans to shell entities linked to its promoters and can no longer service its borrowings.
A hedge fund with thin capital but enormous leveraged bond-arbitrage positions collapses when a sovereign default makes all its trades move together.
A derivatives trader who also books his own settlements hides mounting futures losses in a secret account until they exceed the firm's capital.
A broker-dealer funds mortgage assets with overnight repo and uses quarter-end accounting tricks to temporarily shrink its reported leverage.
A listed fintech's profits come from opaque overseas processing partners, and auditors cannot confirm over a billion euros supposedly held in escrow.
A customer makes many cash deposits just below the mandatory reporting limit, spread across different branches and consecutive days.
An employee buys shares just before an unannounced acquisition using confidential information from work.
The finance team receives an email that looks like it is from the CEO urgently instructing a wire transfer to a new account.
A caller impersonating the bank convinces a customer to read out the one-time passcode that was just texted to their phone.
A fraudster ports a victim's mobile number onto a new SIM to intercept authentication codes and drain the linked account.
Recently opened accounts receive funds from many unrelated senders and immediately forward the money onward in a chain.
Importers and exporters mis-invoice shipments, over- and under-stating goods values to move value across borders.
Funds are routed through multiple shell companies across several jurisdictions to obscure where the money originally came from.
Loans are taken out using fabricated identities that blend real and fake personal details, then deliberately defaulted.
A purchasing manager quietly steers contracts to a vendor he secretly owns, at inflated prices.
An attacker uses a spoofed HR request to change an employee's direct-deposit details and redirect their salary.
A company pays a routine supplier invoice whose bank account details had been quietly altered to a fraudster's account.
A seller is tricked into scanning a QR code that debits money from their account instead of crediting it.
A web of companies with no real operations move money between one another to create the appearance of legitimate business.
Stolen card numbers are used for a rapid series of online purchases without the physical card being present.
A company inflates its revenue and conceals liabilities to hit targets and mislead investors and lenders.
